paper_title: Tackling Discrepancies in Trade Data: The Harvard Growth Lab International Trade Datasets
research_answer: Global trade data is far less reliable than most people assume. Only about 10% of all country pairs represent the 'best case' where both partners report a trade flow and their numbers agree within 25% of each other. The situation breaks down dramatically from there: roughly 50% of all possible country pairs show no trade reported by either side, about 9.3% have trade reported only by the exporter, and 10% only by the importer. Most strikingly, among the flows where both countries do report, nearly two-thirds — about 66.7% — show discrepancies greater than 25%, a threshold large enough to account for legitimate differences like freight and insurance costs. This means that truly consistent, jointly-reported bilateral trade data is the exception rather than the rule in international trade statistics.
supporting_quotes: The best-case scenario, in which both partners report and the discrepancy is small, applies to only about 10% of all country pairs.
In 2010, approximately 50% of all possible country pairs fell into this category [neither country reported any bilateral trade].
the flows shown in blue and orange represent cases where trade was reported only by the exporter (9.3%) or only by the importer (10%), respectively.
nearly two-thirds of the jointly reported trade flows exhibit discrepancies greater than 25%, shown in green.

data_points.metric: Share of country pairs with ideal bilateral reporting (<25% discrepancy)
Share of country pairs with no trade reported by either side
Share of flows reported only by exporter
Share of flows reported only by importer
Share of jointly reported flows with >25% discrepancy
